coredata.dc:title: Ultra-sensitive and stable N-doped carbon dots for selective detection of uranium through electron transfer induced UO2 +(V) sensing mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qun
Zhang, Hengyang
Yu, Dongmei
Qin, Wei
Wu, Xiaohong
coredata.dc:description: Unintended leakage of toxic and radioactive uranyl ion (UO2 2+) ions poses high harmful to human health and environment, hence its monitoring and detection is of utmost significance. Here, we developed a mild synthetic route to prepare stable N-doped carbon nanodots (CDs) with special hydrophilicity functional groups and graphitic N by polymerization and carbonization of novel norfloxacin precursor with nitrogen heterocyclic structure. The morphology, chemical structure, fluorescent properties, sensing competition and selectivity behaviors of CDs-UO2 2+ were systematically analyzed. Controllable experiments including low and high concentration CDs-UO2 2+ and another type of carbon dots (TEA-CDs) with low graphitic N amount using triethanolamine (TEA) precursor without nitrogen heterocyclic structure were designed, which further confirmed the effective coordination and interaction mechanism between CDs and UO2 2+ that graphitic N in CDs induced the formation UO2 +(V) by electron transfer. It is very suitable for on-site and real-time monitoring of UO2 2+ with an ultra-fast response time (∼20 s) and low detection limit of 20.38 nM (4.7 μg L−1), which is lower than the permissible limits (30 μg L−1) defined by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). More importantly, different form the liquid sensor, solid sensors (printed test strip and hydrogel) were also successfully employed to monitor UO2 2+ targets quickly, broadening the potential application for the advanced image encryptions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Uranium
Carbon dots
Nitrogen heterocyclic structure
Sensing mechanism
Electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The preparation and interior and surface characterization of CDs
Stable uranyl(V) complex interaction mechanism between UO2 2+ and CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots (CDs). (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs. (b) TEM (the inset shows that “Tyndall effect” of CDs aqueous dispersion) and (c) high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CDs (the inset shows that lattice spacing of CDs). (d) Size distribution of CDs and XPS spectra of (e) the C 1 s, (f) O 1s and (g) N 1s region of CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical properties and photostability of laboratory-synthesized CDs. (a) The normalized UV–vis absorption spectra (black line) and normalized fluorescence (FL) excitation (blue line) and emission (red line) spectra of the CDs in water. The excitation and emission wavelength of CDs are 330 nm and 420 nm, respectively. The insets present the corresponding CDs water dispersion under visible and UV lamp (365 nm). (b) The fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 240 nm to 410 nm with a 10 nm step. (c) Fluorescence stability of CDs under different pH conditions and (d) different irradiation times (λ ex = 320 nm). C CDs = 0.01 mg mL−1. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical response of UO2 2+ to CDs. (a) Effect of different metal ions for CDs fluorescence intensity. (b) Cation selectivity and competitiveness experiments toward different metal ions in CDs water dispersions. Cyan pillars, green pillars and cyan striped pillars are fluorescence intensity of blank CDs, CDs+17 cation analytes (0.5 mM) and CDs + UO2 2+ (0.5 mM) + other 16 cation analytes (0.5 mM). (c) Fluorescent digital photographs of blank CDs and CDs+17 cation analytes (under 365 nm wavelength). (The digital photographs that correspond to the sequence of cation selectivity experiments in Fig. 3b). (d) Fluorescence emission response of CDs with increasing the concentration of UO2 2+ (0–0.5 mM) in water and (e) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of CDs and UO2 2+ concentration (0–10 μM, λ ex = 320 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of UO2 2+, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (b) Fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and CDs + UO2 2+. (c) The effect of adding different concentrations of UO2 2+ on the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (C CDs = 0.01 mg mL−1). (d) The cyclic voltammetry curve of CDs. (e) Band gap calculated from the UV–vis absorption spectrum. (f) Plot of F0/F versus various concentrations of UO2 2+. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a, b) FT-IR, (c) XPS spectra of CDs before and after quenching. (c) High-resolution XPS spectra of U 4f (d), N 1s (e) and O 1s (f) for CDs before and after quenching. (g) Electron transfer mechanism and possible uranium detection mechanisms of CDs and UO2 2+. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photographs under UV light (365 nm). (a) Test strips, (b) SEM image of fluorescence nanofibrous films of air-laid paper, (c) the logo of Harbin institute of technology (HIT) was painted on test paper, (d) Illustration of sensing hydrogel. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Ultra-sensitive and stable N-doped carbon dots for selective detection of uranium through electron transfer induced UO2 +(V) sensing mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, MIIT, Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, MIIT, Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Key Laboratory of Materials Behavior and Evaluation Technology in Space Environments, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, Heilongjiang, 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Key Laboratory of Materials Behavior and Evaluation Technology in Space Environments
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Key Laboratory of Materials Behavior and Evaluation Technology in Space Environments, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150001, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150001, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150001, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Unintended leakage of toxic and radioactive uranyl ion (UO2 2+) ions poses high harmful to human health and environment, hence its monitoring and detection is of utmost significance. Here, we developed a mild synthetic route to prepare stable N-doped carbon nanodots (CDs) with special hydrophilicity functional groups and graphitic N by polymerization and carbonization of novel norfloxacin precursor with nitrogen heterocyclic structure. The morphology, chemical structure, fluorescent properties, sensing competition and selectivity behaviors of CDs-UO2 2+ were systematically analyzed. Controllable experiments including low and high concentration CDs-UO2 2+ and another type of carbon dots (TEA-CDs) with low graphitic N amount using triethanolamine (TEA) precursor without nitrogen heterocyclic structure were designed, which further confirmed the effective coordination and interaction mechanism between CDs and UO2 2+ that graphitic N in CDs induced the formation UO2 +(V) by electron transfer. It is very suitable for on-site and real-time monitoring of UO2 2+ with an ultra-fast response time (∼20 s) and low detection limit of 20.38 nM (4.7 μg L−1), which is lower than the permissible limits (30 μg L−1) defined by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). More importantly, different form the liquid sensor, solid sensors (printed test strip and hydrogel) were also successfully employed to monitor UO2 2+ targets quickly, broadening the potential application for the advanced image encryptions.

Carbon nanodots (CDs) derived from norfloxacin precursor with nitrogen heterocyclic structure exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity for UO2 2+ detection because graphitic N in CDs induced the formation UO2 +(V) by electron transfer.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Up to 2050, the International Atomic Energy Agency predicts that total energy-generating capacity of nuclear power for global demand may increase by as much as 82%. Due to the intensive industrial utilization such as nuclear facilities and weaponry [1,2] and the fundamental actinide catalytic research via the modulation of its unique 5f electronic structure [3–5], nuclear waste pollution is virtually inevitable. As a predominant fuel of nuclear energy, Uranium (U) with the primary oxidation states U(IV, V, VI) is the heaviest naturally occurring element on the earth and has known abundant geological reserves (4.5 million tonnes) and more than 4 billion tonnes in the ocean [1,2]. Under ambient conditions, most stable soluble ionic form of uranium in water is linear uranyl ion (UO2 2+) species containing a strong dioxo unit, which has brought a series of toxic and radioactive pollution concerns to water, soil, atmosphere and ecosystem [6–10]. Therefore, United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) defined the contamination limit of uranium in drinking water is 30 ppb [6,7,10]. Although many commercial analytical techniques including inductively coupled plasma mass and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy had been established for uranium detection, but they are high cost, time-consuming and require sophisticated instrumentation and displayed insufficient selectivity and poor accuracy [9]. Recently, many new type materials with different sensing mechanisms have been explored for the selective fluorescence recognition and binding of UO2 2+, for instance, inorganic metal-based semiconductor quantum dots [6], inorganic-organic hybird nanocomposites [7], small-molecule-based fluorescent organic complexes [8,9] and stable sp2 carbon-conjugated covalent organic frameworks [10]. However, metal-based chemical sensor faces potential metal ion leakage risk and toxicity in synthesis and detection process. Furthermore, the high cost organic polymer sensors require high stability under extreme conditions (acid, base, temperature and radiation).

The fascinating metal-free carbon dots (CDs) containing abundant surface functional groups as binding sites have aroused enormous interest owing to their superior merits such as low cost, the tunable optical properties, chemical inertness and good biocompatibility. CDs with good solubility in water are also a ideal candidate for developing printable sensing materials coated on papers or other substrates. In general, CDs were derived from a “decomposition-polymerization-carbonization” process using carbon-containing organic molecules including citric acid (CA), Urea, different C–H chains organic amine and cationic surfactant cetylpyridinium bromide (CPB) consisting of a quaternary nitrogen atom and heterocyclic ring at its head and 16 carbon atoms at its tail under various fabricated conditions [11]. Recently, special carbon precursor Levofloxacin with nitrogen heterocyclic structure was developed to produce CDs containing N-related triplet state with green phosphorescence emission [12]. The advance dynamic information encryption [12], photo/electrocatalysis [13] and chemical sensing for the detection of special substances including iodine, curcumin [14], Cu2+ and ClO − [15] and promethazine (PMZ) [16] had been explored. Additionally, the detection of biomolecules such as Glutathione (GSH) in the presence of different-size Cdots@MnO2 nanocomposite systems has been explored, which is responsible for a large number of diseases in humans [17,18]. Addition of GSH into the Cdot@MnO2 sensing system would cause a redox reaction Mn4+-Mn2+ between GSH and MnO2, which turns on/enhances the fluorescence properties of Cdots synthesized through using ascorbic acid as the precursor. It should be noted that CDs for UO2 2+-sensing were rare due to the lack of suitable carbon precursors. So far, there are two reports about CDs prepared by citric acid and ethylenediamine [19] and 3,4-hydroxypyridinone surface functionalized routes [20] for the detection of UO2 2+. However, to the best of our knowledge, the low detection limit is hardly realized and the sensing mechanisms and interaction chemistry between N-doping carbon dots (CDs) and f-block cations are not revealed, let alone CD-based fluorescence test strip and hydrogel sensor.

Herein, we are aimed at develop a facile and fast method for the fabrication of CDs and it has an ultra-fast response time (∼20 s) and an ultra-low detection limit of 20.38 nM (4.7 μg L−1) for on-site and real-time monitoring of UO2 2+. Simultaneously, detailed designed experiments had been done in order to a better understanding of the sensing mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation and interior and surface characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In our present work, special carbon precursor Norfloxacin with nitrogen heterocyclic structure was selected. Initially, a high temperature and pressure hydrothermal process was adopted to fabricate the deep blue emissive CDs at 200 °C for 6 h. With regards to the “bottom-up” strategy, the soft branched short chain organic molecule precursors are sealed and reacted in hydrothermal reactor and underwent the polymerization process forming chain compound intermediates by a condensation reaction at high temperatures. Subsequently, the further dehydration and cross-linking among these organic molecules facilitated the formation of the abundant C, N, O, F, H-based surface and carbon core, which mainly inherited from the precursor. Hence, the cross-linked network skeleton of N-CDs appeared (Fig. 1 a), leading to the unique chemical surface states and the interior aromatic microstructures containing graphitic N domains. Tan et al. [12] and Su et al. [16] also proposed similar structural depiction. It is reported that different synthesis methods and carbon precursors result in formation of CDs which comprises of amorphous or crystalline carbon structures that are sp2 hybridized graphene nucleus or diamond like structures containing sp3 hybridized carbon [11]. In our case, the special nitrogen heterocyclic structure precursor can be polymerized and carbonized to form an extended conjugation intermediates of the nitrogen heteroaromatic π system in the carbon core by covalent, non-covalent or other interactions during the carbonization process, that is the carbonized cores could be the interior aromatic microstructures containing graphitic N domains. Fig. 1b and c depicted that the prepared CDs appeared partial monodispersed and spherical dot-shape features, with an average diameter of approximately 3–4 nm. The “Tyndall effect” of CDs dispersion aqueous further confirmed the quantum dot behaviour (the inset of Fig. 1b). Tyndall effect is the phenomenon of light scattering when a light beam passes through a colloid in the range of 1–100 nm in diameter. The lattice spacing of CDs was measured to be 0.21 nm from Fig. 1c, corresponding to the graphite (100) planes, indicating that CD's topological structure was stable sp2 domain within the graphitic core structures [12,16], which is different from the amorphous phase CDs by solid state thermal method [14]. The particle sizes of the CDs were mostly (more than 70%) narrow distributed in the range of 3.0–3.8 nm (Fig. 1d), indicating that the synthetic process is high level of size selectivity. Moreover, the two typical broad peaks of X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. S1) centered at 23° and 43° corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of graphite were assigned to the interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm and 0.21 nm, respectively [3]. It seems that satisfactory crystallinity of CDs is favor to obtained in hydrothermal surrounding which is known as a “black box” chemical reaction under the high temperature and pressure condition in a closed system [5]. Then, the norfloxacin organic carbon source is evenly mixed in aqueous solution, and undergoes a series assembly, polymerization, crosslinking and carbonization evolutionary behaviors, so the CDs product with graphite carbon core has high purity and good dispersion [16].

Moreover, a series of abundant polar hydrophilicity functional groups (e.g., O–H, N–H, C–O and CO, etc.) and non-polar sp3/sp2 carbon (C–C/CC) of CDs were identified by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray surface photoelectron spectra (XPS) analysis. FTIR spectra were associated with the stretching vibration and the bending vibration modes of various chemical bonds, which could help to determine all molecular types and structures. As illustrated in Fig. S2, the vibration absorption bands of hydroxyl (-OH) and amino (-NH2) groups at 3400 cm−1 and 3275 cm−1 had been observed. Similar results have been observed. For instance, the absorption bands at 3400 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of the carbon dots (CDs) synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal treatment of levofloxacin correspond to stretching vibrations of hydroxyl(OH) groups [12]. The stretching vibrations of 3441 cm−1, and 3240 cm−1 demonstrated the existence of –OH and –NH2 on the surface of N-CDs originated from polyethylenimine (PEI) [16], which may be attributed to the different chemical environment in the synthesis system. And three peaks at 1710 cm −1, 1614 cm−1 and 1565 cm −1 were related to the existence of CO, CC/CN stretching vibration and N–H bending vibration, respectively [13]. Meanwhile, the absorption band at ∼1300–1500 cm−1 can be interpreted as the nitrogen heterocycle, which was inherited structural features from the norfloxacin carbon source [12]. The XPS identification of CDs were further conducted to analyze the surface element compositions and chemical states of materials. The full XPS spectrum presented in Fig. S3 shows four major peaks centered at ≈ 286 eV, 531 eV, 400 eV and 687 eV, indicating that the presence of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and F 1s, respectively. Furthermore, the atomic ratios of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and fluorine elements were calculated to be 70.8%, 10.76%, 14.18%, and 4.98% in Table S1. Specially, it is worthy to note that the C and N amount (70.8% and 14.18%) in our sample is approximately 1.5 and 2.7 times of the previous reported CDs containing C (48.2%) [16] and N (5.3%) [12] using polyethylenimine and Levofloxacin carbon precursor respectively, indicating high C and N amount in the prepared CDs.

The high-resolution XPS survey scan of C 1s can be deconvoluted into four component peaks, corresponding to C–C/CC (sp3/sp2 carbon) at binding energies of 284.7 eV, C–N/C–O (sp3 carbon) at 285.7 eV, CO/CN at 286.7 eV, and C–F at 288.6 eV (Fig. 1e) [12,15,16], further verified the existence of the graphitic sp 2 carbon core structure. The O 1s spectrum has three characteristic peaks at 530.7, 531.9 and 533.2 eV, ascribing to the presence of CO, O–H/C–O, and –COOH groups (Fig. 1f) [12,15]. Similarly, the existence of pyrrolic N, graphitic N and N–H with binding energies of 399.4 eV, 400.5 eV, and 401.3 eV can be easily distinguished in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) [12]. Obviously, the FTIR and XPS results supported the existence of the graphitic N core structure in CDs due to the presence of carbon source with nitrogen heterocyclic units.

To deeply understand the optical property, the UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDs aqueous were measured. Fig. 2 a showed that the CDs exhibited the strongest emission peak at around 420 nm when excited at 330 nm. Moreover, the CDs water dispersion in the inset of Fig. 2a displayed a uniform and transparent system under visible, while emitted a brilliant blue fluorescent at all angles under 365 nm UV excitation. The UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 2a (black curve) showed two adsorption peaks: the first peak at ∼265 nm were contributed by the complex π→π* transitions of the aromatic CC bonds in the sp 2 domains of the CDs core. Meanwhile, the second wider band ranging from 290 to 360 nm (centered at 328 nm) may be assigned to a mild n→π* transitions of nitrogen or oxygen-based groups (CO, C–O, CN and N–H) of sub-fluorophores located on the CDs surface [15,16]. Afterward, the fluorescence behaviors of CDs were also investigated by increasing the excitation wavelength from 240 to 410 nm with 10 nm step, as shown in Fig. 2b, red-shifted phenomenon was observed, showing the CDs possessed an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior due to their diverse traps in carbon dot [16]. Similarly, Tan et al. and Yang et al. also reported that excitation-dependent fluorescence of CDs were explained by the presence of multifluorescence centers in the CDs [12] and diverse surface defect states [15]. Interestingly, the excitation-dependent PL features were found in traditional inorganic phosphors [21]. As a matter fact, it is well known that the excitation-dependent fluorescence properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are dependent upon various factors including the variation in size, surface edge defects, quantum confinement effect (QCE), conjugated sp2 p-domains, and zigzag edge sites [22,23]. In the literature, the excitation-dependent and excitation-independent PL behavior of GQDs are still in debate. Among the different sizes, shapes, or surfaces of GQDs developed by different methods, some of them still have the same excitation-dependent PL properties. In the present work, the surface defect is the main factor for the excitation-dependent PL behavior rather than the different particles size because they have approximate uniform sizes.

For practical sensing applications of CDs, good water solubility and fluorescence stability under various chemical detection conditions were quite necessary [10]. Consequently, the fluorescence stability of CDs with numerous oxygen-rich functional groups (e.g. C–O, –OH and CO, etc.) on the surface under different pH and time conditions were studied. As depicted in Fig. 2c, it is can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was gradually rising with increasing pH (pH = 2 to 7), but changes inversely in the range of pH = 8–11. Of course, stronger acidic (pH < 1) and alkaline (pH > 12) conditions will lead to the plunged fluorescence intensity, which could be attributed to the impact of protonation effect [15,20] and cause the destruction of the surface functional groups [12]. In addition, the fluorescence intensity was almost no significant change and remained favorable photostability when the prepared CDs solution was continuously scanned under 320 nm excitation for 3 h (Fig. 2d), which is quite suitable for a wider range of applications in most natural conditions. As a matter of fact, even though extensive experimental studies have been performed on synthesizing and characterizing carbon quantum dots, their fluorescent mechanism still remains unclear. Previous research showed that the fluorescence has alternately been assigned to intraband and interband transitions, surface states, or ligand-metal charge transfer states etc. [24]. Two possible models for the origin of the photoluminescence were proposed by Aikens's research group: a “solid state” model and a “molecular” model, where the high and low energy bands correspond to interband and intraband (HOMO-LUMO) transitions, respectively. Meanwhile, theory study reported that the fluorescent decay dynamics are complex, which could be due to the presence of several closely spaced energy levels and geometric relaxations in the optimized excited states and impart remarkable effects on the energy levels of the frontier orbitals [24]. Specially, the electronic structure can be explained from the viewpoint of chemical and physical aspects, the energy difference between the bonding and anti-bonding molecular orbitals (HOMO-LUMO gap in a molecule) is related to the band gap E g between the valence and conduction bands (VB and CB) in a semiconductor solid material [25].

Unique surface of CDs can quickly and sensitively identify an ion and present only the unique color (eg. fluorescence quenching), which is particularly ideal for biological and chemical detection to avoid strong optical interference from other ions. Considering the outstanding fluorescence properties and stability of CDs, their potential applications in the detection of common metal ions such as UO2 2+, Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Li+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ (same testing conditions, 0.5 mM), were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence signal slight increased for Al3+ and is weakly quenched in the presence of other competitive ions including Ni2+, Cr3+, Fe3+ etc.. Similarly, Wang's research group had also found such phenomenon for Al3+ and Cr3+ [20]. Compared to other cations, the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm decreased obviously when UO2 2+ was only present in CDs water dispersions (Fig. 3a and c), proving trace UO2 2+ ions as fluorescence quencher have high specificity and clear “signal-off” response for CDs. In order to present better comparison among many kinds of metal ions, the UO2 2+ ion was designed to place in the middle, so the metal ions were separated into two groups in Fig. 3c. In addition, the practical detection of CDs in complex environment, namely the competition experiment of UO2 2+ ions, was performed to evaluate the capability of a detection system. As shown in Fig. 3b (cyan striped pillars), after adding UO2 2+ ions (0.5 mM) into CDs water dispersions containing other ions (0.5 mM), the fluorescence at 420 nm were significantly quenched, indicating that excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability in competing multi-metal ion mixtures which is attributable to the specific affinity between UO2 2+ and highly accessible binding sites of CDs [10]. Next, in order to study the relationship between concentration of UO2 2+ and fluorescence intensity of CDs, the fluorescence titration experiment was carried out by the successive addition of ∼1.25 μM of UO2 2+ quencher (0.5 mM) to CDs water dispersions gradually (Fig. 3d). A sharp leap of the quenching ratio of fluorescence intensity was observed in a low uranium(VI) concentration range, indicating a fairly low detection limit for uranium(VI). Fig. 3e showed that the fluorescence intensity value of CDs decreased gradually linearly (R2 = 0.9978) by increasing the concentration of UO2 2+ (0–10 μM) and the detection limit for UO2 2+ was determined to be as low as 0.02038 μM (20.38 nM, 4.7 ppb) based on detection limit calculation equation (3) σ/k [20] (where σ represents the standard deviation of the signal (n = 3), and k represents the slope), which is far lower than the current maximum level for uranium (30 ppb) of UO2 2+ in drinking water regulated by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). Our value is slight bigger than that of the detection limits of the Tb-FAP/agar for UO2 2+ (7.95 nM, 1.85 ppb [7] and 6.7 nM, 1.56 ppb) of carbon-conjugated fluorescent covalent organic framework (COF) by Cui et al. [10], and is far smaller than that of most small-molecule-based fluorescent probes (10−6-10−8 M, 1 μM–0.01 μM, 2.33 ppb–233 ppb) [9] and 0.71 ppm (710 ppb, 3.05 μM) and 6.53 ppb, 28 nM of CDs [19,20]. Table S2 showed the comparative detection limits values for UO2 2+ by using different sensing probes and techniques. In a word, the above results implied that as-fabricated CDs could be used as a highly sensitive, selective on-site and real-time fluorescence sensor to monitor UO2 2+.

The detection mechanisms for UO2 2+ was investigated in detailed. At present, five PL quenching mechanisms had mainly been devoted and elucidated for the design of CD-based optical biosensors: namely, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE) and static quenching effect (SQE) [14]. It was found that an obvious overlap between the UV absorption band of UO2 2+ and the emission band of CDs in Fig. 4 a, which may causes the weaken excitation and quenching result originated from FRET, PET, IFE [14]. Unlike FRET and PET quenching, strictly speaking, the PL of CDs is not “quenched” in an IFE process, it is simply “blocked” from the detector. That is the emitted light of donors (i.e., CDs), before reaching the detector, is absorbed by the acceptors (i.e., target analytes), causing decrease of PL intensity of CDs. As for DQE and SQE, it is well known that the fluorescence lifetime decay analysis is used as one of the most effective methods to distinguish dynamic and static quenching [14]. Biexponential fits were used to quantify the decay traces of CDs and CDs + UO2 2+ systems according to the equation, which exhibited a superposition of fast and slow dynamics.
(1)
f i t = A +
B 1
exp
−
t
τ 1
+
B 2
exp
−
t
τ 2
where A, B1, and B2 are constants and the two decay components τ 1 and τ 2 represent a bulk component (slow) and a surface component (fast). The average PL lifetime (τ avg) in Fig. 4b can be calculated by the following equation:
(2)
τ
a v g
=
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2

τ avg reflects the overall emission decay behavior. As a result, the average fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and CDs + UO2 2+ were 4.695 ns and 4.685 ns, respectively. The nearly constant fluorescence lifetime value indicated that the quenching mechanism caused by UO2 2+ was probably SQE due to the complexation of UO2 2+ and CDs. A similar SQE feature was observed at the detection of curcumin using CDs by Tang et al., the average fluorescence lifetimes were 25.12 and 24.99 ns before and after adding the curcumin, indicating the quenching mechanism will not be from dynamic quenching (DQE) and FRET [14].

Interestingly, it seems that the energy transfer process was always found in the system of rare earth (RE) compounds [7,8]. For instance, the energy transfer process occurred between the Tb3+-doped fluorapatite/agar (Tb-FAP/agar) nanocomposite sample and Cu2+, Cr3+ ions, leading to the fluorescent quenching phenomenon [7]. Harvey et al. suggested that the unique photophysical properties of UO2 2+ allow energy transfer process to provide a highly selective method of UO2 2+ detection using Europium(III) Complex [8], which is the first example of lanthanide complex triggered by uranyl-(VI) ions. The appearance of two new excitation bands (λ em = 613 nm) at 430 and 320 nm was observed upon addition of UO2 2+, which clearly suggested that the energy transfer from UO2 2+ to Eu3+ [8]. Combination of the facts that the absence of new excitation bands and constant PL lifetime, energy transfer process can be ruled out in our present work. Of course, the PL lifetime is a complex process and critically associated with the different excited states and specific surface condition [13]. As shown in Fig. 4c, with increasing of UO2 2+ concentration, the UV–vis absorption intensity at 265 nm and 328 nm showed different degrees of enhancement and red shift, indicating that the new non-fluorescent or weak fluorescent complex was formed between the UO2 2+ and CDs surface. Furthermore, the color of the sensor system changed from colorless to yellow in the inset of Fig. 4c, which could provide visual detection of UO2 2+ semi-quantitatively with the naked-eye.

Subsequently, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels were calculated to elucidate the nonradiative electron-transfer mechanism. The E red obtained from the cyclic voltammogram of CDs was −0.28 eV (Fig. 4d) and the related empirical formulas were the following:
(3)
E HOMO = − e
( E ox + 4.4 )
(4)
E L U M O = − e
( E r e d + 4.4 )
(5)
E H O M O = E g - E L O M O
where E ox and E red separately represent the onset of the oxidation and reduction potential and E g is the optical energy band gap. Therefore, the E LUMO of CDs was calculated to be −4.12 eV. Analyzing the band gap (E g) of CDs based on absorption spectra, and the E g value of the CDs was calculated to be 3.59 eV estimated from the Tauc plot (Fig. 4e), which is slight bigger than that of N-CDs (E g = 3.33 eV) by Su et al. [16] and smaller than that of NCDs (E g = 3.66 eV) by Tang et al. [14]. The E HOMO value of CDs was calculated to be −7.71 eV. With regard to uranyl ion (UO2 2+), Jiang's research group showed excited UO2 2+ is a superior oxidant and the E ox and energy gap of UO2 2+ were +2.6 eV and 2.76 eV, respectively [4,26]. Thus, the E HOMO and E LUMO of UO2 2+ were calculated to be −7.0 eV and −4.24 eV, respectively. Additionally, the E red of UO2 2+ ions was calculated to be −0.16 eV, which was well in line with the theoretical redox potential value [26]. Therefore, when UO2 2+ ions are combined with CDs, where an energy level distribution diagram is presented in Fig. 5 g, the excited state electrons on the E LUMO of CDs are thermodynamic favorable to transfer to the E LUMO of UO2 2+, which restricted the electron radiation transition and resulted in system's electron transfer (ET) quenched fluorescence. In fact, solvent and ligand had an influence on the energy gap in the case of the UO2 2+-H2O complex, the lowest excited energy level of [UO2]2+ species is T1(3Φg) and the next two higher excited states are T2(3Δg) and S1(1Φg), respectively reported by Ghosh et al. [27]. Furthermore, Li et al. explained that [UO2(NO3)3]- is the colorless U(VI) solution at low concentration [28] due to the level of O 2p in ligand NO3 − as the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) is much lower in energy.

In addition, the quenching type could be validated by the standard Stern-Volmer plot with a good linear correlation within a certain concentration range [16], and were shown in Fig. 4f.
(6)
F 0/F = 1 + K sv[Q]
Wherein, F 0 and F represent the 420 nm peak fluorescence intensities without (only CDs) and with (CDs + UO2 2+) the presence of quencher, respectively, [Q] is the molar concentration of the UO2 2+, and K sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. It is found that a good linear trend of (F 0/F) versus the UO2 2+ working dynamic range from 0 to 7.5 μM, and the calculated K sv value was 5.08 × 104 L mol−1, further demonstrating that the quenching mechanism might be attributed to static quenching SQE [13]. The obtained quenching constant (K sv) of our product is closed to that of the HOPO-CQDs for UO2 2+ 9.496 × 104 L mol−1 [20], smaller than that of Tb-FAP/agar sample 2.47 × 105 M−1 for UO2 2+ [7]. Taken together, based on the above analysis, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs for UO2 2+ ion detection originates from the synergistic effect of the SQE, IFE and PET effect, which formed the uranyl ions-CDs complex via the formation of chemical bonds, and caused the accelerated fluorescence signal to turn off.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stable uranyl(V) complex interaction mechanism between UO2 2+ and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the effective coordination and interaction mechanism between CDs and UO2 2+ were further investigated by FT-IR and XPS analysis. As depicted in Fig. 5, when a trace amount of UO2 2+ was added, the stretching vibration bands (3275 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1) of N–H and O–H of the CDs significantly weakened after adding UO2 2+. And the absorption peaks at 1710 cm−1 related to the existence of CO stretching vibration were red-shifted, meaning the formation of CDs-UO2 2+ complexes (Fig. 5a and b) [12,13]. Primarily, the IR peak at 928 cm−1 (black line) can be attributed to the characteristic antisymmetric vibration of OUO and in comparison with that in UO2(NO3)2·6H2O (∼960 cm−1) and covalent organic framework (COF) (916 cm−1) [10], further confirms that strong interactions exist, while the appearance of another new peak at 565 cm −1 signifies the formation of additional U–O bond, which is possibly owing to U→O contact between UO2 2+ ions and CO units. With regards to XPS analysis, compared to the original CDs, two obvious new U 4f peaks (in the forms of U 4f7/2 and U 4f5/2) emerged in the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 5d) revealing the existence of uranium in the sample after quenching. Furthermore, the high-resolution U 4f XPS spectrum of sample was divided into four-unit moieties: U5+ (U 4f7/2, 381.2 eV), U6+ (U 4f7/2, 382.1 eV), U5+ (U 4f5/2, 391.7 eV), U6+ (U 4f5/2, 392.7 eV) respectively, indicating that the U in the system exists in U5+ and U6+ forms [29], and the ratio of U5+: U6+ is 1.67, which is attribute to the partly reduction of U6+ to U5+ through rich-electron in CDs. Comparing the N 1 s binding energy peaks in the two samples, the peak graphitic N located at 400.5 eV moved 0.22 eV to a higher binding energy after treatment with UO2 2+, indicating a new binding mode happened between CN and UO2 2+ because the 5f orbitals has high oxidation state U6+ [3]. However, the N 1 s peaks of pyrrolic N at 399.4 eV show no shift after treatment with UO2 2+, revealing that the pyrrolic N did not bind to UO2 2+. Comparing the O 1 s peaks (Fig. 5e and f), it is clearly observed that the peaks of CO, O–H/C–O at 530.7 eV, 531.9 eV moved 0.2 eV to a higher binding energy after treatment with UO2 2+, respectively. Specially, based on the above analysis, our present binding mode is similar to the amino and hydroxyl groups embedded in sp 2 carbon-conjugated fluorescent covalent organic frameworks which were considered to be specific affinity uranium-binding groups [10].

Controllable experiments that CDs with high concentration by adding UO2 2+ was carried out to understand the sensoring process, which is similar to amplified the chemical reaction on the molecular level compared to the above low concentration CDs-UO2 2+ detection. Interestingly, increasing the UO2 2+ concentrations form 100 μL–200 μL, the yellow-brown color solution was change to stable dark red-brown color (Fig. S4), which is the characteristic color of U(V)-complexs and could keep stable for several months. Recently, Rahul et al. observed and identified that red-brown color U(V)/dipicolinic acid (DPA) complex in an aqueous medium. Sufficient amount of U(V) is present which is ascribed to the electronic and steric effects and strong complexing ability of DPA stabilizing the unstable oxidation state U(V) in aqueous medium to prevent disproportionation of U(V) into U(VI) and U(IV) [30]. Uranyl(V) (UO2 +) is reported to have a very limited range of stability in aqueous solution where it disproportionates to the more stable uranyl(VI) and uranium(IV) aqua species in air. It is demonstrated that uranyl(V) can be stabilized by aminocarboxylate ligands in environmental conditions [31], indicating nitrogen-based ligands are favorable to produce U(V) compound. Combined the graphitic N peak shift condition of XPS results, this process in our present work enables the CN bond to convert into a C–N bond, which breaks the large conjugated system inside the graphitic N structure, thus quenching the whole fluorescence of CDs. The N in the CN electron donor underwent electron transfer and formed UO2 + by C–N activation, which is similar to the catalytic process using UO2 2+ [4,32]. In comparison, in order to further demonstrate the special role of nitrogen heterocyclic structure precursor, carbon dots (TEA-CDs) using triethanolamine (TEA) without nitrogen heterocyclic structure as carbon source were evaluated whether the existence of the quenching effect of UO2 2+. The morphology, particle size, composition of elements, chemical bonds and functional groups of samples of TEA-CDs were characterized by a X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Figs. S5–S9). Compared to CDs with high graphitic N amount derived from norfloxacin precursor with nitrogen heterocyclic structure, a series of abundant surface N, O-related groups (e.g., O–H, N–H, etc.) of TEA-CDs was verified, but rich-electron the graphitic N was few in TEA-CDs using triethanolamine (TEA) without nitrogen heterocyclic structure. The fluorescence spectra indicated UO2 2+ has almost no influence on the fluorescent emission of TEA-CDs derived from the TEA precursor (Fig. S10), suggesting the special importance of carbon sources. Therefore, based on the above analysis, it seems that the softer nitrogen-based ligands facilitate disproportionation with the lower oxidation state of uranium and analogously the harder oxygen-based ligands with the higher oxidation state of uranium. To be more precise in our case, it is concluded that the nonbonding electrons of graphitic N in our designed CDs possess the electron-doping effect and induce the presence of U(V) chemical valence configurations for the UO2 2+ detection. As a matter of fact, the stability of uranyl(V) as a function of pH, ionic strength, complexing ligands and atmospheric O2 condition should be examined thoroughly through advanced experimental techniques, for instance, spectro-electrochemical methods, extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) and so forth. Further studies on uranyl-CDs mixture system chemistry are on-going in our laboratory.

The CD-based paper sensor, benefiting from its simplicity and rapidity, has a potential application for the visual detection of analytes. Furthermore, hydrogels as water-based soft materials hold great promise for sensing and biological applications owing to their unique merits including high permeability, tunable physical properties and biocompatibility. Based on the easy to operation, tiny size, high stable and amazing optical properties under solvent-free conditions, CDs was evenly printed on test strip nanofibrous films and doped into fluorescence hydrogels to produce strong plastic and portable solid sensors for aqueous UO2 2+ detection, which could provide great convenience and efficiency for instant detection. The process of uranyl adsorption on the test strips and transparent hydrogels induced bright blue fluorescence to dark, which can be readily observed by the naked eye on various metal ions (Fig. 6 a, d), such as UO2 2+, Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Li+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+ (under the same test conditions, 60 mg/L) due to excellent selectivity and sensitivity fluorescence quenching. The nanofibers exhibited short response time due to their small diameters (<10 μm) and a large specific surface area (Fig. 6b). In addition, the logo of Harbin institute of technology (HIT) was painted on test paper and were almost invisible under daylight. Under the excitation of UV at 365 nm, these patterns displayed the bright blue fluorescence, where every letter and Chinese character could be distinctly identified (Fig. 6c), thus broadening the potential application for the advanced image encryptions and hiding the specific information. It is reported that (polyethyleneimine-2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid (PEI-DA) derived CDs doped test strips, nanofibrous films and PEI-DA-doped hydrogels, enabled the quick detection of Cu2+ in the environment [15] and N-CDs was employed as the dual-emissive ink for image encryption [16]. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first report that the CD fluorescence test strip nanofibrous films and hydrogels for the sensing of UO2 2+ in the environment with low costs, easy productions and storage facilitation.

In summary, we have successfully developed a strongly blue fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) with abundant special hydrophilicity functional groups and graphitic N by polymerization and carbonization of novel norfloxacin precursor with nitrogen heterocyclic structure. The as-designed CDs exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward trace UO2 2+ real-time detection in aqueous solutions with an ultra-fast response time (∼20 s) and low detection limit of 20.38 nM (4.7 μg L−1). More interestingly, the sensing mechanism is elucidated to be the synergistic effect of the SQE, IFE and PET where nonbonding electrons of graphitic N in CDs induced the formation UO2 +(V) by electron transfer. In addition, it is first reported that CDs doped test strips, nanofibrous films and hydrogels, enabled the quick detection of UO2 2+ in the environment with low cost, simple preparation and good plasticity, which may broaden the avenue of advanced intelligence encryptions and ion detection.

Qun Wang: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Resources, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Hengyang Zhang: Chemical synthesis, Characterization, Data curation, Writing – original draft, spectroscopy. Dongmei Yu: Validation, Formal analysis. Wei Qin: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Xiaohong Wu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51572060, 51671074 and 51702067).
